coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon dots from Momordica charantia L. for rapid and effective sensing of p-aminoazobenzene in environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Su, Xiaoyan
Chen, Letian
Liu, Huanjia
Zhang, Chunyuan
Liu, Jiali
Hao, Jiayi
Shangguan, Yu
Zhu, Guifen
coredata.dc:description: p-Aminoazobenzene (pAAB) is a hazardous azo dye that causes considerable harm to human health and the environment. The development of novel and sensitive sensors for the rapid detection of pAAB is in high demand. In this study, a simple fluorescent sensor for pAAB detection is designed based on carbon dots (CDs) which are prepared using green carbon source of Momordica charantia L. via a facile hydrothermal approach. The fluorescence spectra of CDs exhibit considerable overlap with the absorption band of pAAB, and the fluorescence is specifically suppressed in the presence of pAAB ascribed to the inner filter effect. Good and wide linearity is observed in the pAAB concentration range of 0.01–12.5 μg mL−1 with a lower detection limit of 3.9 ng mL−1. The established method achieves good results with a rapid analysis of pAAB in different practical water and soil samples. The as-constructed fluorescent sensor provides a simple, rapid, economical and eco-friendly platform and possesses prospective applications for the effective, selective and sensitive detection of pAAB in the environmental field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
p-Aminoazobenzene
Fluorescence quenching
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of pAAB in environmental samples
Characterization and optical properties of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of CDs and the fluorescence detection of pAAB based on the inner filter effect.
(A) TEM images of the CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of the CDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (black line), excitation spectrum (blue line) and emission spectrum (red line) of the CDs. (B) FL spectra of the CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Effect of pH values on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. (D) Effect of the concentration of NaCl solution on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. (E) The stability of the CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs + pAAB. (B) the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, pAAB, and CDs + pAAB. (C) The emission spectrum (black line) and excitation spectrum (red line) of the CDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (blue line) of the pAAB. (D) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs, CDs + pAAB. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) The optimization of the CDs concentration. (B) The optimization of the pH value. (C) The optimization of the incubation time.

(A) The fluorescence responses of the fluorescent sensor upon the addition of different concentrations of pAAB. (B) A linear correlation of F0/F-1 values versus the concentration of pAAB over the range from 0.01 to 12.5 μg mL−1.
Selectivity of the fluorescent sensor for pAAB over other analogous compounds (single) and the mixture of pAAB with other analogous compounds (mixture).
Comparison of different methods for the detection of pAAB.

row.entry: static quenching and foster energy resonance transfer

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: pAAB-MIP-PGE: pAAB- molecularly imprinted polymers -modified pencil graphite electrode Au@PS-OH substrate: Au@hydroxyl-functionalized polystyrene substrate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recoveries for pAAB in environmental samples.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon dots from Momordica charantia L. for rapid and effective sensing of p-aminoazobenzene in environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan, 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control
Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan, 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: p-Aminoazobenzene (pAAB) is a hazardous azo dye that causes considerable harm to human health and the environment. The development of novel and sensitive sensors for the rapid detection of pAAB is in high demand. In this study, a simple fluorescent sensor for pAAB detection is designed based on carbon dots (CDs) which are prepared using green carbon source of Momordica charantia L. via a facile hydrothermal approach. The fluorescence spectra of CDs exhibit considerable overlap with the absorption band of pAAB, and the fluorescence is specifically suppressed in the presence of pAAB ascribed to the inner filter effect. Good and wide linearity is observed in the pAAB concentration range of 0.01–12.5 μg mL−1 with a lower detection limit of 3.9 ng mL−1. The established method achieves good results with a rapid analysis of pAAB in different practical water and soil samples. The as-constructed fluorescent sensor provides a simple, rapid, economical and eco-friendly platform and possesses prospective applications for the effective, selective and sensitive detection of pAAB in the environmental field.

ce:section.ce:para: Azo dyes with the –NN– chromophore, are extensively applied in clothing, cosmetics, rubber, paper, plastic, textile and other industrial fields (Aljerf, 2018). Dye wastewater is considered the most polluting effluent among all industries and as much as 200,000 tons of dyes escape treatment processes each year (Chequer et al., 2013). Most azo dyes especially aromatic amine compounds are toxic and can pose large threats to humans and other organisms (Cui et al., 2020; Lipskikh et al., 2018; Sha et al., 2016; Tropp et al., 2020). p-Aminoazobenzene (pAAB) is a kind of prohibited textile aromatic amine compound with carcinogenicity (Li et al., 2020b). NOx, a thermal decomposition product from pAAB, is also highly toxic to human health (Nehme et al., 2020). In recent years, an increasing number of anthropogenic activities have promoted the release of pAAB into the environment, which further leads to an increasing hazard to humans and ecosystems (Ma et al., 2017; Mori et al., 2015; Waghmode et al., 2019). Hence, the sensitive detection of pAAB is beneficial for reducing the threat to human health (Chequer et al., 2011). Currently, a wide variety of analytical methods have been explored to sense pAAB including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Pielesz et al., 2002), ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) (Yang and Weisz, 2018), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Aznar et al., 2009), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Balçık et al., 2020), and MS (Yang et al., 2009). However, most of these methods always suffer from considerable shortcomings of high cost, cumbersome instrumentation, complicated and time consuming procedures. Therefore, it is desirable to explore a suitable analytic method for rapid, simple and feasible sensing of pAAB.

Fluorescent sensors have exceptional performance in terms of low cost, fast response, technical simplicity and specific recognition (Tan et al., 2020a). Furthermore, carbon dots (CDs) have been utilized as fluorescent sensors, which not only have excellent optical properties but are also more environmentally friendly. Compared with organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, CDs have significant merits of photobleaching resistance, excellent biocompatibility and low cost (Wei et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019). Recently, many fluorescent sensors based on CDs have also been designed and reported (Devi et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a; Sun and Lei, 2017), where CDs are believed to be an ideal candidate for establishing a novel fluorescent sensor for efficient determination of pAAB. Dynamic quenching, static quenching, photoinduced electron transfer, inner filter effect (IFE) and Foster energy resonance transfer (FRET) are some common fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs (Zu et al., 2017). Compared with other mechanisms, IFE has the distinctive merits of simplicity, good flexibility and no covalent linkage (Aggarwal et al., 2018). Fluorescent sensors based on IFE have attracted considerable attention and many analysis methods have been constructed to detect environmental pollution (Chen et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2016; Tan et al., 2020b; Zhao et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2021). Huang et al. (2019) synthesized N, P, S tri-doped CDs and further employed the CDs as a fluorescent sensor for rapidly sensing Cr6+ attributed to the IFE. Cr6+ as the quencher had a maximum absorption located at 350 nm while CDs as the fluorescence material displayed two excitation peaks at approximately 360 nm and 420 nm (Bharathi et al., 2020; Du et al., 2018). There were large overlaps between the absorption spectrum of Cr6+ and the excitation spectrum of CDs, which caused Cr6+ to quench the fluorescence of CDs through IFE. The developed method was successfully applied to the analysis of Cr6+ in living cells and practical waters. Hu and coworkers (Hu and Gao, 2020b) designed another fluorescent sensor based on Se, N-codoped CDs from selenourea and o-phenylenediamine for sensitive detection of crystal violet. The experimental results showed the absorption band of crystal violet was at 450–650 nm and exhibited a beneficial overlap with the emission of CDs (Zhao et al., 2019). In addition, the unchanged fluorescence lifetime excluded FRET and indicated that IFE was the main quenching mechanism (Wang et al., 2020). The detection limit could be as low as 3.0 ng mL−1. Similarly, Du et al. (2020) employed N-doped CDs to realize the selective detection of VB12 derived from the IFE. The absorption band of VB12 revealed a large proportion of overlap with both the emission and excitation spectra of CDs and the almost constant fluorescence lifetimes also indicated that the quenching effect was attributed to the IFE rather than FRET.

In this case, we used Momordica charantia L. and sodium borohydride as a green precursor and reductant to prepare CDs. As exhibited in Fig. 1 , CDs with excellent fluorescent properties were obtained through a facile and simple hydrothermal method. In particular, the prepared CDs have an optimal excitation wavelength at 332 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength at approximately 418 nm. We further found that the fluorescence of CDs can be especially quenched by pAAB originating from IFE. The absorption band of pAAB as the quencher exhibits an efficient spectral overlap with the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs as the fluorescent substance. The obtained CDs are successfully constructed as an effective fluorescent sensor for label-free, rapid and sensitive determination of pAAB. Moreover, the developed method demonstrates favorable analytical performance with a rapid response and ultraselectivity for pAAB detection in soil and various environmental water samples.

Momordica charantia L. was purchased from a local supermarket. pAAB, p-hydroxyazobenzene (pHAB), azobenzen (AB), p-methoxyazobenzene (pMAB), o-aminoazotoluene (oAAT), Sudan orange G (Sundan G), Sudan Ⅰ, and sodium borohydride were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sudan Ⅱ and Sudan Ⅳ were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sudan Ⅲ was obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Pure water was from Wahaha Co., Ltd. (Henan, China).

The morphologies and sizes of the CDs were determined on a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, Hillsboro, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a PerkinElmer 983 infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Norwalk, USA). UV–Vis measurements were collected with a T6 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, Beijing, China). All fluorescence spectra were taken in a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Palo Alto, USA). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were obtained from an FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Edinburgh, British).

The preparation was conducted via a one-step hydrothermal approach. First, Momordica charantia L. was cut into thin slices without seeds and then placed in a vacuum drying oven overnight at 60 °C. Furthermore, the slices were ground into powder and sieved through a forty-mesh sieve. Then, 20 mL of pure water was added to 0.6 g of powder and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min. After 30 mg of sodium borohydride was added, the mixture was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave and further heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone filter membrane to remove the large particles and dialyzed through a regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO: 1 KDa) for 3 h after reaction. The obtained CDs were preserved at 4 °C at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1.

In a standard detection route, 100 μL CDs solution and various amounts of pAAB solutions were added and further diluted to 2 mL with pure water in a centrifuge tube. The mixture was transferred to a 2 mL quartz cell for quantitative determination after shaking thoroughly. Fluorescence measurements were performed under 332 nm excitation. To evaluate the selectivity, we prepared similar substances solutions including pHAB (0.1–0.5 μg mL−1), AB (0.1–1 μg mL−1), pMAB (0.1–1 μg mL−1), oAAT (0.1–0.5 μg mL−1), Sudan G (0.1–0.5 μg mL−1), Sudan Ⅰ (0.1–1 μg mL−1), Sudan Ⅱ (0.1–0.5 μg mL−1), and Sudan Ⅲ (0.1–0.5 μg mL−1), Sudan Ⅳ (0.1–1 μg mL−1). Moreover, the interfering compounds in both a single solution and a mixture with pAAB (0.1 μg mL−1) in the CDs were all investigated by the same process as pAAB detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of pAAB in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of pAAB in four environmental samples was utilized to assess the practicability of the constructed fluorescent sensor. The three water samples were from Renmin Park, Muye Lake, and Wei River and filtered through 0.45 μm microporous membranes to remove insoluble substances. The soil sample was collected from a farm in Xinxiang. The sample was first air-dried, and then ground to powder for sieving through a 100-mesh screen. Furthermore, 5.0 g of accurately weighed dry sample was extracted with 100 mL of acetonitrile for 12 h, followed by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained supernatant was evaporated to near dryness and later dissolved in pure water. The determination operation was the same as the abovementioned pAAB detection, except that the environmental sample solution was substituted for pure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs was provided by the TEM. As exhibited in Fig. 2 A, the obtained CDs are monodisperse and exhibit spherical or subsphaeroidal shapes. The mean diameter is approximately 8.5 nm, as determined by analyzing the sizes of 80 nanoparticles via Nano Measurer 1.2 software. The surface functional groups of CDs were further investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. It is evident from Fig. 2B that the broad characteristic band centered at 3410 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of N–H or O–H. The obvious absorbance band located at 2931 cm−1 is assigned to the C–H stretching vibration and the peak at 1710 cm−1 indicates the existence of CO (Aljerf and Nadra, 2019). The sharp peak at 1384 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of O–H or the stretching vibration of C–H (Long et al., 2012). The two absorption bands with peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 790 cm−1 further show the presence of the N–H bond (Mohammed and Omer, 2020). The clear peak at 1099 cm−1 is from the C–O stretching vibration (Hu and Gao, 2020a; Wang et al., 2019). The FTIR signals demonstrated that the prepared CDs possessed various hydrophilic functional groups containing hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups. These functional groups are beneficial to further strengthen the water solubility and fluorescent properties of the as-prepared CDs (Lv et al., 2020; Qiu et al., 2020).

The optical characteristics of CDs were further researched via the powerful tools of UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 A (black line), the observable absorption peak at approximately 252 nm is associated with the π-π* transition of the CC bond. There is another shoulder peak at 325 m that may be related to the n-π* transition of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups (Jiang et al., 2018; Tao et al., 2018). Under the optimum excitation wavelength of 332 nm (blue line), the optimal emissive wavelength of CDs is located at 418 nm (red line). It is obvious from Fig. 3B that the obtained CDs had excitation-dependent fluorescent properties while the excitation wavelength varies ranging from 260 nm to 370 nm. This property indicated that the fluorescence characteristics probably relied on the surface state (Jiao et al., 2019).

Fig. 3C describes the fluorescence fluctuations of CDs under various pH values. The prepared CDs display no conspicuous fluorescence changes at pH values ranging from 5 to 11, while the fluorescence intensities present two opposite phenomena at ranges of 1–5 and 11–14. This is conducive to fluorescent detection with stable and strong fluorescence behavior over a broad pH range. Ionic strength is one of the major variables that impacts chemical reactions and sensing applications. The effect of ionic strength is demonstrated in Fig. 3D. The fluorescence of CDs exhibits inconspicuous changes until the concentration of NaCl reaches 2.5 mol L−1, which illustrates that the obtained CDs possess good tolerance to high ionic strength and promising utilization under physiological conditions (Liu et al., 2017). Additionally, Fig. 3F shows that the gained CDs exhibited rather invariable fluorescence for two months, which is also favorable for detection analysis. The above results illustrate that the prepared CDs express excellent photostability and can served as good-performance fluorescent sensors.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence of CDs at approximately 418 nm is dramatically quenched in the presence of pAAB. To illuminate the exact quenching mechanism, a series of experiments were performed. It is obvious from Fig. 4B that different absorption peaks do not appear in this sensing system compared with the UV–Vis spectra of CDs, pAAB, and CDs + pAAB which suggests that there is no relevant chemical reaction between pAAB and CDs, and that no complex is formed. This result excludes the effect of static quenching which can cause some changes in the UV–Vis spectra of CDs derived from the appearance of the nonfluorescent ground-state complex (Zu et al., 2017). In Fig. 4C, the absorption spectrum of pAAB effectively overlaps with not only the excitation but also the emission spectra of CDs, indicating the quenching mechanism is probably attributed to IFE or FRET. As a common and powerful tool, fluorescence lifetime measurements were undertaken to further distinguish the two described detection mechanisms. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is 8.16 ns while that of CDs in the presence of pAAB is 7.93 ns, and the fluctuation of less than 1 ns induces no visible change as displayed in Fig. 4D. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs declines when quenching stems from FRET. This result eliminates the probability of the FRET quenching mechanism, and therefore the quenching mechanism of CDs is mainly ascribed to IFE (Li et al., 2019, 2020c; Wang et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2019).

To achieve the satisfactory detection efficiency toward pAAB, we further designed and conducted systematic experiments to optimize some significant experimental factors including CDs concentration, pH value of solution and incubation time. A lower CDs concentration can offer better analysis properties and higher sensitivity, while a higher concentration may afford a broader detection range, so a suitable CDs concentration was first investigated in the detection process. Fig. 5 A shows that the F0/F-1 value first increased and later decreased as the CDs concentration increased. The largest value was acquired with a CDs concentration of 50 μg mL−1. Hence, 50 μg mL−1 was applied as the proper CDs concentration for the detection of pAAB. The pH value of the solution also plays a critical role in the detection process. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the F0/F-1 value rose obviously as the pH increased and later diminished with the highest value at pH 7.0. These results suggested that strong acidity or alkalinity conditions may lessen the effect of the IFE between CDs and pAAB. Therefore, a suitable pH was kept at pH 7.0 for subsequent experiments. As displayed in Fig. 5C, the value of F0/F-1 remained steady in 10 min which demonstrated that this fluorescent sensor could realize the rapid detection of pAAB. Therefore, 0 min served as the proper incubation time for the follow-up experiments.

As mentioned above, the prepared CDs exhibited strong fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 332 nm. The fluorescence was significantly quenched with the addition of pAAB, which indicated that a novel fluorescent sensor based on the obtained CDs could be constructed to quantitatively determine pAAB. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence of CDs decreased progressively as the pAAB concentration was augmented under the optimum reaction conditions. Simultaneously, the relevant calibration curve of F0/F-1 versus pAAB concentration is presented in Fig. 6B. A good linear response with a squared regression coefficient of 0.9995 is found in the pAAB concentration range of 0.01–12.5 μg mL−1 with the regression equation F0/F-1 = 0.2809 × C pAAB (μg mL−1) - 0.0043. The limit of detection (LOD) was 3.9 ng mL−1 using the 3σ/s rule, where σ represents the standard deviation of twenty blank sample measurements, and s is the slope of the linear calibration curve. Ma et al. (2017) designed another novel sensor for the stable and selective sensing of pAAB by coupling a molecularly imprinted polymer with interfacial potentiometry with double poles. The detection range was from 0.197 to 197 ng mL−1 and the LOD was 0.135 ng mL−1. This method had a lower LOD, but the preparation of the sensor was relatively complex and expensive. Zhang et al. (2016) prepared a novel Au@hydroxyl-functionalized polystyrene substrate and utilized it to realize the highly selective detection of pAAB through a surface-enhanced Raman scattering technique. The preparation of the Au@hydroxyl-functionalized polystyrene substrate was complex. Furthermore, the related reagents and materials were somewhat toxic and expensive. This method had a linear range of 0.5–7.0 μg mL−1 with an LOD of 50 ng mL−1. Li and coworkers (Li et al., 2020b) proposed another analytical method for pAAB detection based upon copper nanoclusters. This method was relatively economical and environmentally friendly, but it had a higher LOD of 284 ng mL−1. A comparison between the present work and the abovementioned methods was performed and the detailed results are exhibited in Table 1 . Compared with the aforementioned previous strategies for pAAB detection, this present method is capable of providing higher detection sensitivity and wider detection range with more simplicity, economy and environmental friendliness.

According to the abovementioned experiments, this fluorescent sensor can be utilized for a sensitive and quantitative analysis of pAAB. However, a big challenge for pAAB detection is not merely in sensitivity but more importantly selectivity due to the complexity of environmental samples. Hence, subsequent experiments were conducted to further assess the selectivity of the fluorescent sensor toward pAAB. First, experiments spiking 0.1 μg mL−1 pAAB and other potential interferences containing 0.5 μg mL−1 pHAB, 1 μg mL−1 AB, 0.5 μg mL−1 pMAB, 0.5 μg mL−1 oAAT, 0.5 μg mL−1 Sudan G, 1 μg mL−1 Sudan Ⅰ, 0.5 μg mL−1 Sudan Ⅱ, 0.5 μg mL−1 Sudan Ⅲ, and 1 μg mL−1 Sudan Ⅳ were conducted. As exhibited in Fig. 7 (single), these competitors caused little fluorescence quenching compared with pAAB. Furthermore, mixtures of pAAB and the abovementioned analogous compounds were added to CDs solutions, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were measured. Fig. 7 (mixture) clearly shows that the quenching efficiency has no significant change in the presence of other analogous compounds. The aforementioned results powerfully reveal the explored fluorescent sensor has admirable selectivity for pAAB and can be exploited to specifically identify pAAB.

To verify the practicability and feasibility of our proposed method, various concentrations of pAAB were added to environmental samples. No pAAB was detected in water and soil samples. The spiked experimental results are listed in Table 2 , and the recoveries of pAAB ranged from 95.8% to 100.1%. Additionally, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of environmental samples with different concentrations of pAAB were less than 4.2%. These results clearly reveal that the built method is applicable for determination of pAAB in practical samples and that the constructed fluorescent sensor offers an excellent platform for the detection of pAAB in the environmental field.

A novel kind of fluorescent CDs is innovatively fabricated through facile hydrothermal treatment of the natural source of Momordica charantia L. More importantly, the obtained CDs possess brilliant photostability and can serve as a fluorescent sensor for rapid and sensitive determination of pAAB. The fluorescence of CDs is remarkably quenched by pAAB derived from IFE. The feasibility of this fluorescent sensor for pAAB detection has been successfully illustrated in real water and soil samples with satisfactory recoveries. The constructed fluorescent sensor with simplicity and cost efficiency has the potential application for highly sensitive and efficient detection of trace pAAB in the environmental field.

Yongli Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Xiaoyan Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Project administration. Letian Chen:Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Project administration. Huanjia Liu: Writing – review & editing. Chunyuan Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Jiali Liu: Investigation, Software. Jiayi Hao: Data curation, Software. Yu Shangguan: Data curation, Software. Guifen Zhu: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Formal analysis, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42007204), Science Foundation of Henan Normal University (No. 5101219170129, 2021PL23), Key Scientific Research Project Plan of Henan Province (No. 20A610007), Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011) and Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Henan Normal University (No. 2020JQ05).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Secondary inner filter effect allows extremely efficient pure white light emission by spatially separated organic fluorophores

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2021
